prior.title: IPCC Second-Order Draft Review Period Opens for Special Report on Climate Change and Cities
prior.summary: The IPCC has opened registration for the Second-Order Draft (SOD) review of its Special Report on Climate Change and Cities, accepting expert and government comments from May 8 to July 3, 2026. Expert registration closes June 26, 2026. This is a critical stage in the IPCC's rigorous assessment process, with the final report scheduled for March 2027 release. The report will provide comprehensive guidance on climate impacts, risks, adaptation and mitigation strategies for urban areas worldwide.
prior.full_brief:
# IPCC Special Report on Climate Change and Cities — Second-Order Draft Review

**Research Summary | MONITORING**

---

# 1. What the Research Found

The IPCC has opened the Second-Order Draft (SOD) expert review period for its Special Report on Climate Change and Cities (SR Cities), the first report scheduled for publication in the seventh assessment cycle (AR7). This is a forward-intelligence event: the review period is active now, the draft content — including the first Summary for Policymakers (SPM) — is available for qualified expert review, and the March 2027 publication target is a stated schedule, not an estimate.

**Phase and status.** The SOD review period opened 8 May 2026 and closes 3 July 2026. *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/

**Expert registration deadline.** Registration for expert reviewers closes 26 June 2026. *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/ This is independently confirmed: "Registration closes on 26 June 2026 at 9.59pm GMT." *Source: UK Government IPCC SR Cities SOD Expert Review Guidance, UK Department for Energy Security & Net Zero, 6 May 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-expert-review/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-details-and-how-to-apply

**Publication target.** "Scheduled for release in March 2027, the Special Report on Climate Change and Cities will be the first IPCC report published in the seventh assessment cycle. It is also the only special report in the current cycle." *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/

**Outline governance.** "The report's outline (Decision IPCC-LXI-5) was agreed [by] the Panel during its 61st Session held in Sofia, Bulgaria from 27 July to 2 August 2024." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/ The Sofia plenary confirmed: "Panel's approval and publication of this Special Report are scheduled for March 2027." *Source: IPCC 61st Plenary Press Release, IPCC, 2 August 2024. https://www.ipcc.ch/2024/08/02/ipcc-approves-outlines-of-the-first-two-reports-in-the-seventh-assessment-cycle/

**Prior review scale.** "During the Expert Review from October to December 2025, the First-Order Draft of the Special Report on Climate Change and Cities received more than 32,000 comments from 1,365 registered Expert Reviewers around the world." *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/

**Production timeline (sourced milestones):**
- Scoping meeting: April 2024, Riga, Latvia
- First Lead Author Meeting: 10–14 March 2025, Osaka, Japan
- Second Lead Author Meeting: 21–25 July 2025, Mombasa, Kenya
- Third Lead Author Meeting: 12–16 January 2026, Oslo, Norway
- FOD expert review: October–December 2025
- SOD expert review: 8 May – 3 July 2026
- SOD registration closes: 26 June 2026
- Publication: March 2027

*Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

**Scientific leadership.** "The Special Report will be developed under the joint scientific leadership of Working Groups I, II and III with support from the Working Group II Technical Support Unit." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/ The Scientific Steering Committee is chaired by IPCC Vice-Chair Diana Ürge-Vorsatz. Working Group II Co-Chairs Winston Chow and Bart van den Hurk are leading production. *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

**Scope of the report.** "The agreed outline of the Special Report on Climate Change and Cities includes trends, challenges and opportunities for cities in a changing climate, the actions and solutions to reduce urban risks and emissions, how to facilitate and accelerate change in the context of cities, and solutions by city and types and regions." *Source: IPCC 61st Plenary Press Release, IPCC, 2 August 2024. https://www.ipcc.ch/2024/08/02/ipcc-approves-outlines-of-the-first-two-reports-in-the-seventh-assessment-cycle/

The scoping meeting participant expertise areas confirm the report addresses — among others — "supply chain, vulnerability, losses and damages, risk management" and "transportation systems and mobility services" and "energy systems, infrastructure" and "governance, policy, institutions, planning and finance" including "barriers and enabling conditions for urban climate finance" and "environmental, social and governance reporting." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

**Methodology note.** The SOD is a peer-reviewed draft, not a published finding. Content visible to registered expert reviewers is unpublished and subject to change. The brief does not characterise the draft's substantive findings because the draft text is not a public primary source available for span-grounding. What is grounded here is the process, the schedule, the governance structure, the scope of the outline, and the policy context — all of which are published facts.

---

# 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

The SR Cities report is explicitly designed to feed into the 2028 UN Global Stocktake process — the second iteration of the stocktake mechanism under the Paris Agreement. Analytical inference: that connection means the report's findings on urban transport emissions, supply-chain vulnerability, and urban logistics infrastructure are likely to inform national government climate-policy updates in the 2026–2028 window, with downstream consequences for urban delivery regulation, clean-air zones, low-emission zone expansions, and client sustainability reporting frameworks.

Operational implication: for workspaces operating freight movements into, out of, and within major urban centres — across air primary, road secondary, and ocean tertiary modes — the SR Cities report represents the scientific baseline that regulators and city governments will cite when tightening access rules, mandating emissions disclosures for urban freight, and setting clean-vehicle thresholds. The report's scope explicitly includes "transportation systems and mobility services," "critical infrastructure including power, digital communication and transport," and "barriers and enabling conditions for urban climate finance." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

Analytical inference: freight operations serving event, luxury, high-value, and time-critical cargo verticals — which concentrate deliveries in high-density urban cores with the most aggressive clean-air and low-emission zone trajectories — face the most direct exposure to policy tightening that the SR Cities report could accelerate.

Operational implication: the SOD review period is an intelligence window. The draft is currently available to registered expert reviewers, meaning the workspace has nine weeks to gain visibility of the scientific consensus before it is finalised, rather than reacting to the published report in March 2027.

**Re-check window.** Severity is MONITORING. The report is in draft; findings are not final. The re-check trigger is the final report approval and SPM publication, scheduled March 2027. The next substantive intelligence update should be pulled no later than Q1 2027, when the approved SPM will define the publicly citable findings.

---

# 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**Participation window — closing 26 June 2026.** The SOD expert review registration closes 26 June 2026. *Source: UK Government IPCC SR Cities SOD Expert Review Guidance, UK Department for Energy Security & Net Zero, 6 May 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-expert-review/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-details-and-how-to-apply Workspaces in freight-forwarding, logistics, and supply-chain roles with in-house sustainability expertise — or with access to sector-specific technical advisors — can register as expert reviewers and gain early sight of the draft findings in the urban transport, supply-chain vulnerability, and urban governance sections. This is a legitimate, sanctioned channel for early intelligence.

Analytical inference: the competitive advantage here is not regulatory compliance but strategic anticipation — understanding what scientific framing city governments will adopt when updating urban freight access policies, clean-air zone standards, and supplier sustainability requirements in the 2027–2029 period.

Operational implication: the workspace's client conversations on urban delivery capability, last-mile sustainability credentials, and forward regulatory exposure will be more credible if they are grounded in the SR Cities findings rather than the prior AR6 framework. The SPM, once published in March 2027, will be the most-cited scientific reference in urban climate policy discussions for the following five to seven years. Workspaces that have read the draft will have a 9-month lead on competitors who wait for publication.

**Decisions this changes:**
1. Whether to register a qualified team member or external advisor as an expert reviewer before 26 June 2026.
2. How to frame client sustainability briefings for cargo verticals operating in regulated urban corridors — event logistics, fine art, luxury, and high-value shipments concentrated in city centres.
3. Whether to begin tracking urban freight regulatory developments across the workspace's trade-lane cities ahead of the March 2027 publication, rather than reacting afterward.
4. How to engage with city-level clean-air zone consultations that may cite SR Cities findings as the regulatory basis.

---

# 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The IPCC is finalising its first-ever Special Report specifically on climate change and cities, due for publication in March 2027. The workspace is tracking its development. *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/
- The report scope explicitly addresses urban transport systems, supply-chain risk in cities, and governance conditions for urban climate finance — areas that directly shape the regulatory environment for freight serving city-based clients.
- The report will be the primary scientific reference informing city government and national government policy updates in the 2027–2029 period, relevant to clean-air zones, low-emission vehicle mandates, and urban access restrictions.
- The workspace recommends clients assess their supply-chain exposure to urban freight regulatory tightening now, rather than after the report triggers policy action.

**What to avoid saying:**

- Do not characterise the draft's specific findings: the SOD is not a public document, and its content is subject to change before the March 2027 approval session. Any characterisation of draft content that turns out to differ from the final approved text will undermine credibility.
- Do not describe the report as "binding" or as creating direct compliance obligations. IPCC reports are scientific assessments. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether and how any specific city government or national regulator incorporates SR Cities findings into enforceable policy is a legal and regulatory question outside the scope of this brief.
- Do not conflate the SOD review opportunity with participation in policymaking. Expert reviewer status is a scientific peer-review function, not a lobbying or advocacy channel.

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- What proportion of your deliveries route through low-emission zones or urban access-restricted areas today?
- Has your organisation mapped its urban last-mile exposure across the trade lanes you rely on?
- Are your supplier contracts ready to respond to a regulatory tightening in urban freight access post-2027?

---

# 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

The SOD review period yields draft visibility but not published findings. This brief does not resolve:

- What the SR Cities report will specifically find or recommend regarding urban freight, logistics infrastructure, or supply-chain emissions. The draft is unpublished; the approved findings are not available until March 2027.
- How individual city governments or national regulators will translate SR Cities findings into policy. Analytical inference: the policy translation process varies significantly by jurisdiction, political context, and existing regulatory frameworks, and will unfold over the 2027–2030 period.
- Whether the 2028 Global Stocktake process will produce specific freight-sector recommendations. Carbon Brief's analysis identified SR Cities' relevance to the 2028 stocktake, but the specific outcomes of that process are not yet determinable. *Source: Carbon Brief, Carbon Brief, published prior to May 2026. https://www.carbonbrief.org/ipcc-governments-split-on-accelerated-climate-reports-for-next-un-global-stocktake/
- Whether participating as an expert reviewer creates any confidentiality obligations regarding draft content. *Legal Confirmation Required:* expert reviewers accessing the SOD should confirm the applicable IPCC confidentiality and code of conduct requirements before discussing draft content externally.

**Re-check trigger:** March 2027 — SR Cities approval plenary and SPM publication. Set a monitoring pull for February 2027 to capture any pre-approval signals from the plenary session.

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| IPCC SR Cities SOD Expert Review: Details and How to Apply (UK Government) | https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-expert-review/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-details-and-how-to-apply | 2 | Government-level participant guidance confirming registration deadline (26 June 2026) and procedural requirements — authoritative secondary corroboration for the SOD timeline, more specific than the IPCC press release on deadline GMT timestamp. |
| IPCC approves outlines of the first two reports in the seventh assessment cycle (61st Plenary) | https://www.ipcc.ch/2024/08/02/ipcc-approves-outlines-of-the-first-two-reports-in-the-seventh-assessment-cycle/ | 3 | Official IPCC plenary record locking the SR Cities outline and March 2027 approval/publication schedule — provides the governance origin of the timeline; essential for tracing the schedule to a binding panel decision rather than a press release estimate. |
| IPCC Special Report on Climate Change and Cities (dedicated report page) | https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/ | 3 | IPCC's canonical landing page with the full production timeline, author list, Scientific Steering Committee membership, and lead author meeting dates — the single most complete sourced timeline available for the SR Cities production schedule. |
| IPCC Governments Split on Accelerated Climate Reports for Next UN Global Stocktake | https://www.carbonbrief.org/ipcc-governments-split-on-accelerated-climate-reports-for-next-un-global-stocktake/ | 5 | Analytical trade-press account linking SR Cities to the 2028 Global Stocktake — provides the policy relevance context that explains why the report matters beyond its scientific content; no primary-source equivalent for this connection is available in the source pool. |

---



*Specific penalty / enforcement provision not available from primary sources as of June 2026. IPCC Special Reports are scientific assessment documents; they carry no direct compliance penalty on any freight operator. Downstream regulatory consequences are jurisdiction- and policy-specific and are tracked separately.*

---
